coredata.dc:title: Optimization studies of expired mouthwash drugs on the corrosion of aluminum 7475 in 1 M hydrochloric acid: Gravimetrical, electrochemical, morphological and theoretical investigations

coredata.dc:description: The aim of the present work is to study the corrosion inhibition of Al 7475 alloy by an expired mouthwash drug (EMWD) in a 1 M HCl solution. The corrosion rate was optimized by the Doehlert experimental design at various temperatures, concentrations of inhibitor, and rotational velocity. The corrosion behavior was investigated by mass loss, Tafel potentiodynamic polarization, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques. SEM, MOMT, XRD, FT-IR spectrum, and drop wettability measurement were used in characterizations of EMWD and surface morphology. Optimum conditions for corrosion control of EMWD on aluminum were 44.12 °C, 141.71 mL/L of EMWD per liter of acid, and 234.89 rpm. Maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency was 84.717% at higher levels of temperature and inhibitor concentration. It is observed that EMWD follows the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm with negative values of ΔGads o, proposing a stable spontaneous inhibition process. The inhibitor efficiency increased as the inhibitor concentration increased. Moreover, increasing the solution temperature and rotational velocity had an adverse influence on inhibitor efficiency. The results of mass loss were in good agreement with potentiodynamic polarization and EIS. Polarization diagrams revealed that the inhibitor acts as a mixed-type inhibitor. It could be concluded that response surface Doehlert design (RSDD) was adequately applicable in the optimization of corrosion inhibition process variables and that EMWD sufficiently inhibited the corrosion of the aluminum at the studied conditions. Simulation of the EMWD molecules adsorption on the metal surface by quantum chemical theoretical studies was a powerful tool for supporting experimental outcomes.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aim of the present work is to study the corrosion inhibition of Al 7475 alloy by an expired mouthwash drug (EMWD) in a 1 M HCl solution. The corrosion rate was optimized by the Doehlert experimental design at various temperatures, concentrations of inhibitor, and rotational velocity. The corrosion behavior was investigated by mass loss, Tafel potentiodynamic polarization, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques. SEM, MOMT, XRD, FT-IR spectrum, and drop wettability measurement were used in characterizations of EMWD and surface morphology. Optimum conditions for corrosion control of EMWD on aluminum were 44.12 °C, 141.71 mL/L of EMWD per liter of acid, and 234.89 rpm. Maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency was 84.717% at higher levels of temperature and inhibitor concentration. It is observed that EMWD follows the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm with negative values of ΔGads o, proposing a stable spontaneous inhibition process. The inhibitor efficiency increased as the inhibitor concentration increased. Moreover, increasing the solution temperature and rotational velocity had an adverse influence on inhibitor efficiency. The results of mass loss were in good agreement with potentiodynamic polarization and EIS. Polarization diagrams revealed that the inhibitor acts as a mixed-type inhibitor. It could be concluded that response surface Doehlert design (RSDD) was adequately applicable in the optimization of corrosion inhibition process variables and that EMWD sufficiently inhibited the corrosion of the aluminum at the studied conditions. Simulation of the EMWD molecules adsorption on the metal surface by quantum chemical theoretical studies was a powerful tool for supporting experimental outcomes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion is a serious problem that threatens the chemical and petrochemical industries. Annually, corrosion costs billions due to equipment damage and failure (El Ouadi et al., 2020). However, it can be controlled using different methods, such as cathodic protection, corrosion inhibitors, the selection of highly corrosion-resistant materials, etc. Because of their excellent thermal and electrical conductivity, low density, and high strength-to-weight ratio, aluminum and its alloys are widely employed in a variety of industrial applications (Rashid and Khadom, 2020, 2022). Aluminum and its alloys can be self-protected by an oxide layer that forms under certain conditions. The protective layer, however, is lost in extremely acidic and alkaline situations (Rashid et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2021). Acidic solutions (such as HCl) are commonly used in chemical and petrochemical industries for the cleaning, descaling, and pickling of aluminum and its alloys, resulting in severe corrosion hazards and massive economic losses (Fayomi et al., 2019; Hameed et al., 2015). There are numerous strategies for reducing aluminum corrosion. The use of corrosion inhibitors, which have the potential to reduce corrosion, is the most crucial and effective (Abdallah et al., 2022).

Several chemicals and compounds have been employed to prevent the corrosion of aluminum in HCl solutions, such as organic compounds, nonionic surfactant molecules, natural extracts of some plants, and pharmaceutical compounds (Chaubey et al., 2018; Almashhadani et al., 2021). These compounds function by forming an adsorbed layer that adheres to the surface of aluminum, preventing the corrosive solution from damaging it (Alqarni et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2022a). The effectiveness of the inhibitor varies depending on factors such as the properties of the acidic solution, metal nature, temperature, solution velocity, the chemical structure of the inhibitor, etc. Previous research has shown that the presence of heteroatoms (O, N, and S), aromatic rings, and conjugated π bonds in the chemical structures of the additives is essential to the effectiveness of the inhibitor, as it increases when these factors are present. Recently, undesirable, expired prescription medications that have passed their expiration date have been used as corrosion inhibitors (Iroha et al., 2023; Mehta and Yadav, 2023). As mentioned above, the choice of expired drugs for employment as corrosion inhibitors was based on various aspects. In addition, the influence on the environment compared with conventional toxic inhibitors and the fact that they are free of charge are other important factors. Thus, expired drugs, when used as corrosion inhibitors, have to satisfy performance, economic, and environmental considerations. Mouthwash drugs consist mainly of several active ingredients, such as eucalyptol (C10H18O), thymol (C10H14O), menthol (C10H20O) and methyl salicylate (C8H8O3). Fig. 1 shows the chemical structures of the main compounds in the mouthwash. There are currently no reports on using mouthwash as a corrosion inhibitor for aluminum in hydrochloric acid, according to the literature review. In addition, the optimization of the corrosion inhibition process of aluminum in an acidic solution was limited.

The aim of the present work is to optimize the corrosion inhibition process of Al in 1 M HCl using an expired mouthwash drug. The corrosion rate was the objective function or response variable, while temperature, mouthwash concentration, and solution velocity were the factors or independent variables. The objective function was fitted to the second-order polynomial equation, and then optimum conditions were evaluated. Gravimetrical and electrochemical measurements are used for corrosion behavior estimation. SEM, MOMT, XRD, FT-IR spectrum, and drop wettability measurements were used in surface and mouthwash characterizations. Furthermore, quantum chemical studies are used as a theoretical tool to confirm the experimental study.

The AA7475 aluminum alloy specimens with chemical compositions of 90.4 A l, 5.6 Zn, 2.4 Mg, 1.4 Si, and 0.22 Cr were the estimated chemical compositions (in weight percent) used in the present work. After that, keep it until usage. Analysis-grade HCl was diluted with distilled water to create the potent 1 M HCl solution. As a corrosion inhibitor, LISTERINE® COOL MINT expired mouthwash was utilized. The expired LISTERINE® COOL MINT bottles were collected from pharmacies and drug stores in Baghdad markets, Iraq. The compositions contain inactive ingredients (water, alcohol 21.6%, sorbitol solution, flavoring, poloxamer 407, benzoic acid, sodium saccharin, and sodium benzoate) as well as active substances (eucalyptol 0.092%, thymol 0.064%, methyl salicylate 0.060%, and menthol 0.042%). The drug was a solution with soluble chemical compounds. Therefore, the solubility of expired drugs was easy in the corrosive solution.

For mass loss experiments, the rectangular AA7475 aluminum alloy specimens were cut to dimensions of 75 mm × 20 mm × 1 mm, resulting in a total surface area of 31.9 cm2. These specimens were exposed to the 1 M HCl acid solution for 3 h at the desired temperature, inhibitor concentration, and speed. The metal samples were extensively cleaned with sandpaper ranging from 800 to 1500 grit before being submerged in the corrosive solution. After each cleaning, they were rinsed with distilled water, dried, submerged in alcohol, and finally dried with clean tissue. The rate of corrosion was measured in grams per square meter per day (gmd). Each experiment was repeated twice to ensure accuracy and minimize experimental error. The cleaning and experimental procedures were carried out according to ASTM standards (G1-03, G5-87, G59-97, and G102-89).

The corrosion rate was evaluated at different temperatures (40–70 °C), flow conditions (0–1000 rpm), and inhibitor concentrations (50–300 mL/L). The temperature range was within the operating conditions of pickling and industrial processes. While acid cleaning processes were achieved either in static or dynamic conditions, hence the two cases were studied. In addition, considering that for industrial applications, the dosage of a corrosion inhibitor is normally around 1% (Zhang et al., 2022). Therefore, the above inhibitor concentration range (50–300 mL/L, or 0.05–0.3%) was within acceptable limits.

For electrochemical measurements, AA7475 aluminum alloy specimens with an exposed surface area of 1.0 cm2 were employed. Prior to each experiment, the surfaces of aluminum specimens were mechanically polished with different grades of emery paper, degreased with acetone, and rinsed with distilled water. Electrochemical experiments were recorded using a Vertex-One equipment potentiostat device (Netherland). Three electrodes and a 1-Liter cell were used. Working (AA7475 aluminum), calomel (saturated calomel electrode, SCE), and counter (Platinum) electrodes were equipped in the cell and connected to the potentiostat device. Three electrochemical measurements were conducted. Open circuit potential (OCP), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), and electrochemical impedance microscopy (EIS) Open circuit potential (OCP) was observed during immersion of the working electrode for 150 min to check the system stability before PDP and EIS tests. The PDP curves were measured at 250 mV around OCP. The scanning rate was 1 mV/s. A signal amplitude of 10 mV was used for EIS, with a frequency range of 10 kHz to 0.01 Hz.

The Al specimens were immersed in 1 M hydrochloric acid and optimum conditions. The specimens were subjected to an optical microscope, XRD, and FT-IR. The adsorption of EMWD on the Al surface was investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, SPEACTUM-65) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1.

To determine the effective wetting characteristics of Al 7475 alloy, the Creating Nano Tech. Si-Plasma Taiwan model No.: CAM110P Serial No.:113,031,201,804 W Contact angle measuring device was used. The samples were prepared according to the weight loss measurements. Tests were conducted at different EMWD concentrations in 1 M HCl solution droplets above the AA7475 aluminum alloy surface at 44.12 °C.

Doehlert experimental designs were used in the construction of tests. The corrosion rate was evaluated as a function of three independent variables: temperature (F1, oC), EMWD concentration (F2, mL/L), and rotational velocity (F3, rpm). The variables were estimated at different levels, unique center points, and step variations. The number of levels for F1 was 5, 50, and 20, for F2 it was 7, 50, and 150, and for F3 it was 3, 500, and 500, respectively. Eq. (1) was used to convert the real variable to a coded one.

The temperature levels were 30, 40, 50, 60, and 70 °C; the EMWD levels were 0, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, and 300 mL/L; and the rotational velocities were 0, 500, and 1000 rpm. The data were fitted to the second-order model (Eq. (2)). The superiority of the model is demonstrated by the higher significance of the regression coefficient (R2) and the findings of an analysis of variance (ANOVA):

Y stands for the response function (coded corrosion rate), X1, X2, and X3 are the operational coding factors, and ho–h9 are the coefficients of regression. The mathematical and statistical analysis of Eq. (2) were achieved using STATISTIC software, version 10. Nonlinear estimation was used, based on the least-squares Levenberg-Marquardt estimation method. The maximum number of iterations was 1000, the convergence criterion was 1 × 10−6, the p-level was 0.05, and the confidence intervals for parameter estimation were 95%. To investigate the required response, 15 experiments were conducted according to Doehlert experimental design based on employing the response surface methodology (RSM). Eq. Quantum-chemical investigations, which are based on density functional theory (DFT), were conducted using ArgusLab software. Several Hamiltonian methods, such as AM1, MNDO, and PM3, are available. MNDO represents a classical and old-fashioned method. While AM1 and PM3 represent modified methods. In the present work, geometry and energy optimization are dependent on the AM1 Hamiltonian with a maximum of 200 iterations and 10−10 kcal/mol convergence. The calculations were achieved in the gas phase. Eucalyptol, thymol, menthol, and methyl salicylate were studied as active compounds of EMWD. Several quantum chemical parameters are obtained, such as the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), the dipole moment (μ), and the fraction of electrons transferred (ΔN).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Doehlert experimental design and ANOVA analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Table 1 shows a matrix of 15 runs constructed according to the Doehlert experimental design (Hameed et al., 2022). At each run, the corrosion rate was evaluated. The results of the corrosion rate were correlated to the independent variables using a second-order polynomial model (Eq. (2)). Regression analysis based on the least-squares Levenberg-Marquardt method yields two mathematical models in real and coded form (Eq. (2a) and (b)), with a significant correlation coefficient (R2) equal to 0.96.

To check the validity of the models, an analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed, as shown in Table 1. It is clear that all coefficient effects were significant. Fig. 2 shows the Pareto chart for the intercept, individual, square, and interaction effects of variables on the corrosion of AA7475 aluminum alloy in 1 M HCl solution. As shown in Table 2 , the experimental corrosion rate was in good agreement with the predicted one, and the absolute error (
|
ε i
|
)
was relatively low. Optimization of Eqs. (2a) and (2b) by partial differentiation and Cramer's rule produced the optimum conditions. The minimum corrosion rate was 115.85 gmd at 44.124 °C, 141.714 mL/L, and 234.893 rpm for the real variables, and −0.297, −0.154, and −0.604 for the coded variables of temperature, EMWD concentration, and rotational velocity, respectively. The value of the predicted corrosion rate was equal to 115.8503 gmd at the optimal conditions of F1, F2, and F3, and this value is less than the lowest value in Table 1. This may be explained by the fact that Eqs. (2a) and (2b) include the single and combined effects of free agents. While the experimental data was carried out under practical conditions. The improvement of the higher inhibitor concentration was demonstrated by the increase of EMWD particles on the steel surface. The lower level of the rotational velocity range reduces the corrosion attack, depending on its effect on the corrosion mechanism between the exposed medium and the metal surface. Dealing with the lowest temperature level reduces the rate of dissolution of the metal. The linear coefficients (h1, h2, and h3) for the operational free agents (F1, F2, and F3) were 138.22, −331.88, and 244.21, respectively. These clarify that the effect of each factor on the rate of corrosion can be ordered as h3 > h1 > h2, and the rate of corrosion is affected by increasing rotational velocity, then temperature, and decreases by increasing the concentration of the inhibitor. Fig. 3 shows the surface response and contour plots for the interaction of the Al 7475 alloy corrosion rate at optimal conditions. In Fig. 3a and b, the combined effect of temperature and EMWD concentration was illustrated in surface and contour plots, respectively. Similar figures were plotted for the effect of temperature and rotational velocity (Fig. 3c and d) and the effect of EMWD concentration and rotational velocity (Fig. 3e and f).

The suggested mathematical models showed high accuracy. Therefore, these models can be used to study the corrosion behavior of AA7475 aluminum alloy in uninhibited and inhibited 1 M HCl solutions at different conditions. With the aid of models, Table 3 was constructed in terms of corrosion rate as a function of temperature, inhibitor concentration, and optimum velocity. The data in Table 3 has been used as a source for adsorption kinetics and activation studies. The values of corrosion inhibition efficiency from mass loss data (%IEML) and surface coverage (θ) were evaluated using Eqs. (3a) and (3b) (Jasim et al., 2023; Rashid et al., 2023) and listed in Table 3. It is clear that the corrosion rate increased with temperature and decreased with the addition of EMWD. The values of %IEML increased with both; temperature and EMWD concentration. These results agreed with those obtained from experimental design studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature and activation studies
ce:section.ce:para:
Activation parameters, such as frequency factor (A), activation energy (Ea), activation entropy (ΔSa), and activation enthalpy (ΔHa) can be evaluated from the Arrhenius equation (Eq. 4a) and transition state equation (Eq. 4 b) (El Hamdouni et al., 2020; Jasim et al., 2022). Linearization and drawing of these equations are illustrated in Fig. 4 A and B.

Where h is the Plank's constant, R is the gas constant, and N is the Avogadro's number. From equations (4a) and (4b), slopes can be used for the calculation of Ea and ΔHa, while intercepts are used for the calculation of A and ΔSa, respectively. The results are shown in Table 4 . The values of Ea in the presence of EMWD were higher than those for an uninhibited solution. The increase in Ea shows that the EMWD molecules are chemically adsorbed on the Al surface, which indicates the stability of the protective layer on the metal surface at elevated temperature (Benzbiria et al., 2021). The positive values of activation enthalpy indicate an endothermic activation process, while the values of activation entropy are negative, suggesting that the active complex in the rate-determining step represents a gathering rather than disengagement, signifying a lessening in the stochastic conversion from reactants to activating complexes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of EMWD concentration and adsorption studies
ce:section.ce:para:
The adsorption behavior and kinetic parameters are evaluated to understand the mechanism of the inhibition process. Fig. 5 shows different illustrations regarding adsorption studies. The surface coverage, which plays an important role in adsorption studies, is shown in Fig. 5A as a function of inhibitor concentration (C) at different temperatures. It is clear that surface coverage values increased with EMWD concentration due to the increased surface covered by inhibitor molecules at high concentrations. The effect of temperature is also illustrated in Fig. 5A. As temperatures increased, the surface coverage values increased. This may be attributed to the chemisorption of inhibitor molecules on the metal surface. The surface coverage values can be fitted to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm (Eq. (5)) (Ahmed et al., 2019; Damej et al., 2023), as shown in Fig. 5B. The Langmuir adsorption isotherm gives straight lines with high correlation coefficients (R2) and near unity slopes, as shown in Table 5 . This indicated the formation of a monolayer onto the metal surface; each site can hold only a single adsorbed inhibitor molecule; no interaction happens between the inhibitor molecules on different metal sites; the heat of adsorption is not dependent on the number of sites. Furthermore, the value of the adsorption constants (Kads) is evaluated from the intercept of Langmuir lines. The higher the adsorption constant, the higher the adsorption process. Adsorption constants are related to the standard free energy of adsorption (ΔGads) via Eq. (6) (Laaroussi et al., 2022a), and the results are illustrated in Table 5. According to the literature, values of ΔGads greater than −20 kJ/mol are related to chemical adsorption. While other researchers suggested values greater than −40 kJ/mol (Benallal et al., 2023; Jeroundi et al., 2018). According to Table 5, the average value was −31.088, which can be considered in the range of physical and chemical adsorption (i.e., mixed-type adsorption). Other adsorption parameters, such as heat of adsorption (ΔHads) and entropy of adsorption (ΔSads), are evaluated using Vant Hoff's equations (Eq. 13) and Eq. (14), respectively (El-Mokadem et al., 2023). These equations are shown in Fig. 5A and B, respectively. The desorption of water molecules was confirmed by the positive values of ΔSads (Laaroussi et al., 2022b). While the positive values of ΔHads indicate the endothermic adsorption process.

The effect of fluid flow on corrosion is of practical and scientific importance (Merimi et al., 2023). Solution motion can initiate and propagate localized corrosion or enhance uniform corrosion (Mubarak et al., 2023). The effect of velocity on the corrosion rate of the AA7475 aluminum alloy surface in 1 M HCl was studied. Two cases were studied: the corrosion rate as a function of rotational velocity at different temperatures at optimum EMWD concentration (Fig. 6 A) and the corrosion rate as a function of rotational velocity at different EMWD concentrations at optimum temperature (Fig. 6B). It is clear that velocity generally increases the total mass loss rate (%IE decreased). This may be attributed to supplying the corrosive species faster and/or the erosion-corrosion effect. However, for the inhibited acid solution, the effect of velocity may be complex. The corrosion rate depends on flow velocity, which may affect the transport of the inhibitor to the metal surface and the erosion of the inhibitor film at high flow rates (Haruna and Saleh, 2022).

Mass loss results were confirmed by electrochemical measurements. Furthermore, mechanisms of EMWD action on anodic and cathodic regions, corrosion tendency, and metal resistance were addressed. Fig. 7 A shows all electrochemical measurements. Since EIS and PDP experiments are achieved at OCP by pushing the system away from equilibrium, OCP monitoring is crucial for all corrosion research (Mahmood and Khadom, 2016; Abd El-Lateef et al., 2019). Fig. 7A shows the time dependence of the OCP recorded for 150 min of immersion of the AL in 1 M HCl solution without and with the optimum concentration of EMWD. A sharp increase in the OCP was observed during the first 40 min to more positive values. Then, the OCP reached relatively stationary values after 40 min of immersion. The variation and differences in OCP at the beginning of the exposure may be due to the aggressiveness of the HCl solution and the formation of an oxide layer. However, the OCP values at optimum conditions were −710 and −740 mV for inhibited and blank solution, respectively.

The cathodic and anodic potentiodynamic polarization curves of Al in 1.0 M HCl under optimal conditions are shown in Fig. 7B. It is evident that the addition of EMWD inhibited both the cathodic and anodic reactions, suggesting that the drug slows the hydrogen evolution reaction and lessens anodic dissolution. The electrochemical parameters such as corrosion potential (Ecorr), corrosion current density (icorr), anodic Tafel slope (ba), and cathodic Tafel slope (bc) are listed in Table 6 . Furthermore, the percentage of inhibitor efficiency from PDP measurements (%IEPDP) is calculated using Eq. (15) (Talebian et al., 2019) and listed in Table 6.

corr are the corrosion current density in the absence and presence of optimum concentration of EMWD, respectively. The value of Ecorr shift was lower than ±85 mV in presence of EMWD, so it acted as a mixed-type inhibitor with more effect on anodic process (Swathi et al., 2022). In addition, there is no significant change in Tafel slopes after addition of EMWD, which indicates that the cathodic and anodic reaction mechanisms do not change. This means that the EMWD activity is due to the adsorption process.

Further work was carried out to clarify the influence of the EMWD on the corrosion behavior of Al in 1.0 M HCl by EIS measurement. Fig. 7C and D illustrate the Nyquist and Bode, respectively. An equivalent circuit fitting of three elements is used to simulate the measured EIS data (Fig. 7C). The equivalent circuit includes Rs is the solution resistance, which is the resistance between the working electrode and the reference electrode, Rct is the charge transfer resistance, and Cdl is the capacitance of the double layer. Table 6 contains the estimated EIS parameters and percentage of inhibitor efficiencies from EIS measurements (% IEEIS), which were calculated by Eq. (16) (Akinyele et al., 2022).

are the charge transfer resistances in the absence and presence of EMWD, respectively. The Nyquist plot is defined by a single full half-circle that is linked to the charge transfer process, as seen in Fig. 5C. The capacitive loop's diameter increased when EMWD was present, indicating that the corrosion process was being significantly hindered (Motawea and Melhi, 2023). The findings indicated that the presence of EMWD causes Rct to increase and Cdl to decrease. Because the inhibitor molecules gradually replace the water molecules on the metal surface, forming an adsorption layer, an increase in the Rct value improves the effectiveness of inhibition. This demonstrates how the double layer thickness decreases when more of the metal surface is covered by the film. Furthermore, the Bode impedance module and phase angle are shown in Fig. 7D. In the presence of optimal concentration of EMWD, the Bode impedance module and phase angle become greater, which may be attributed to the decrease in Al corrosion rate (Shojaee et al., 2022). Both %IEPDP and %IEEIS were comparable.

The SEM graphs of Al 7475 alloy in 1 M HCl solution at the optimum conditions and 3 h of immersion are shown in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8A shows the clean surface before the corrosion test. The effect of an acidic solution on the alloy is shown in Fig. 8B (uninhibited acid). The damage is observed due to the action of HCl. The alloy surface is improved due to the presence of EMWD (Fig. 8C). A cross-sectional view in the presence of EMWD is shown in Fig. 8D, which illustrates the film thickness. Fig. 9 displays the results of an XRD investigation performed on aluminum in 1 M HCl in the absence and presence of EMWD. From the XRD peaks, it is clear that there were many index planes at 2θ. The figure demonstrates that peak intensity and crystallinity are higher with the EMWD inhibitor. This indicates that the metal surface is likely more protected in the presence of an inhibitor (Haruna et al., 2020).

Fig. 10 shows the FTIR spectra of the liquid EMWD (before drying), the dried solid EMWD (after drying), the dried film removed from the aluminum surface in the absence of EMWD, and the dried film removed from the aluminum surface after 3 h in the presence of EMWD. All FTIR tests are carried out at optimum conditions and after 3 h of immersion. For EMWD, which consists of eucalyptol, thymol, menthol, and methyl salicylate, different spectra are observed at different wave numbers and percentage transmittances. Spectra in the range of 2500–3500 – 3500 cm−1 may be attributed to O–H and C–H stretching. While those in the range of 1400–2000 – 2000 cm−1 may be related to the aromatic rings, CO, and CC, peaks in the range of 1000–1400 – 1400 cm−1 may be attributed to C–C and C–O stretching (Echihi et al., 2021; Chaudhari and Patel, 2019). However, it is clear that the presence of organic functional groups in the EMWD and film removed from the metal surface indicated the formation of a protective layer on the metal surface.

The contact angle test was carried out to diagnose the surface feature of the metal, whether it is hydrophobic or hydrophilic. This test determines the solid substrate's ability to repel liquids (Motawea, 2021). The determination of the contact angle was performed on the aluminum surface immersed in a 1 M HCl solution with and without EMWD. The contact angle of the aluminum surface without an inhibitor was determined to be 25.9°. The results of the examination showed that the surface is characterized by wettability, that is, it has a water-loving characteristic (Alamry et al., 2023). The contact angle increased from 25.9° to 34.7°, 55.5°, 85.7°, 69.3°, 67.1°, and 65.5°–35.9° with additions of different EMWD concentrations, as shown in Fig. 11 . It is possible that the aluminum surface formed a hydrophobic layer due to the presence of an inhibitor molecule, which adsorbed at the maximum contact angle at the optimum of 141.71 mL/L. After that, the surface of the aluminum had formed a hydrophilic layer due to the lack of adsorption of the molecules, which led to a decrease in the values of the contact angles.

Quantum chemical calculations have been widely used to study the reactivity of organic compounds for corrosion inhibition. The inhibitory activities involve the molecular geometry and orbitals of the organic compounds and are correlated with frontier orbital energy. These calculations are recently used as a convincing tool to describe the mode of organic inhibitors adsorption on the metals surface. Calculations are based on density functional theory (DFT), were conducted using ArgusLab software. Eucalyptol, thymol, menthol, and methyl salicylate were studied as active compounds of EMWD. Several quantum chemical parameters are obtained, such as the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), the dipole moment (μ), and the fraction of electrons transferred (ΔN). The optimized structures of eucalyptol, thymol, menthol, and methyl salicylate are shown in Fig. 12 . Furthermore, the HOMO, LUMO, and electron density distributions are also obtained and shown in Fig. 12. The quantum chemical parameters are collected in Table 7 . It is obvious that the distributions are mainly concentrated over the heteroatoms (oxygen) and rings of the compounds. According to the literature, organic compounds with low LUMO energy frequently favor receiving electrons, while those with high HOMO energy favor donating electrons (Singh et al., 2019). The energy gap (ΔE) is another important parameter that can be related to inhibition efficiency. The lower the ΔE value, the higher the inhibitor efficiency (Danaee et al., 2020). According to the energy gap results, the efficiency of the studied EMWD active chemical compounds is eucalyptol > menthol > methyl salicylate > thymol.

The values of ΔN are listed in Table 7. It is known that for ΔN less than 3.6, the inhibition efficiency improved with increasing electron-donating capacity at the aluminum surface (Liu et al., 2022b; Zakaria et al., 2022; Kalkhambkar et al., 2022). Furthermore, the ΔN value is greater than zero, indicating that the inhibitor molecule has a high affinity for donating electrons to the metal surface. Therefore, the expired inhibitor assisted the electron donation process, while the surface of aluminum was an acceptor. The dipole moment (μ) is also collected in Table 7. According to the literature, a low value enhances the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the metal surface, improving inhibitor efficacy (Belghiti et al., 2020). Table 8 shows the other parameters that were calculated depending on HOMO-LUMO energies. The chemical reactivity of the molecule is represented by hardness (η); the higher the hardness, the more stable the molecule. Electrophilicity (ω) is an important index that explains the efficiency of the molecule as a corrosion inhibitor. A high electrophilicity means a good corrosion inhibitor. Back donation (Δback donation) is another significant indictor of the transfer of electrons from the d orbital of a metal to the vacant orbital of an inhibitor molecule. In most cases, the results were in agreement with quantum chemical parameters that were calculated from the molecular optimization of inhibitor molecules.

The adsorption mechanism of EMWD molecules on aluminum surfaces depends on several factors, such as surface conditions, the molecular structure of the inhibitor, the presence of heteroatoms, etc. (Belghiti et al., 2019). It is well known that aluminum can form a protective oxide layer under atmospheric conditions. However, in acidic conditions, the Al oxide layer may be dissolved into its ions according to the following equations (Chen et al., 2021):

The proposed inhibition mechanism model is shown in Fig. 13 . In the absence of EMWD, the aluminum protective layer is destroyed, producing aluminum ions. According to the adsorption isotherm studies, the adsorption of EMWD can be attributed to mixed-mode adsorption, i.e., physical (electrostatic) and chemical adsorption (charge sharing). In the presence of EMWD molecules (eucalyptol, thymol, menthol, and methyl salicylate), the ring and oxygen heteroatoms represented active adsorption sites for chemical adsorption by the unshared pair heteroatoms electron and pi electrons of the aromatic ring. In the physical adsorption case, the protonated inhibitor molecules and other species already present on the surface (such as Cl− ions) can prevent corrosion reactions.

The performance of expired drugs varies according to the types and molecular structures of organic compounds. However, Tanwer and Shukla (2022) conducted a review of research about the applicability of drugs as corrosion inhibitors for metal surfaces. They found that inhibition efficiency for numerous drugs that are used for different metals (steel alloys, aluminum, copper, zinc) in different acids (HCl, H3PO4, etc.) varies from 44.33 to 100%. Many values were around 80 and 90%. Some values were around 70%. Therefore, as compared with the literature, EMWD performance was within the significant protection level.

Expired Listerine mouthwash acts as a significant corrosion inhibitor for aluminum in a 1 M HCl solution. Corrosion inhibition efficiency increased with increasing inhibitor concentrations and temperatures. The formation of a protective layer on the metal surface follows a Langmuir adsorption isotherm. Adsorption was according to physical and chemical modes. Electrochemical polarization curves show that the used inhibitor molecules act as mixed-type inhibitors. Thermodynamic parameters show that the inhibitor under investigation is adsorbed on the aluminum surface by a spontaneous endothermic process. SEM, XRD, and FTIR studies showed the formation of a protective layer. Generally, the outcomes show that when the temperature and inhibitor concentration were greater, the maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency was 84.717%. To forecast the structural and molecular appropriateness of the corrosion inhibition process, a quantum chemical approach is used. However, further work may be required in the future, such as supporting surface morphology and quantum chemical studies with EDX analysis and molecular dynamics simulations, respectively.
